column1: What is a load switch and why is one needed?;A transistor the GPIO controls
column2: which switches the real load current. A GPIO sources only a few mA at 3.3 V
column3: while motors/heaters/solenoids want amperes at higher voltage — the transistor bridges that gap.

column1: Why control a transistor's gate/base instead of driving the load directly?;A GPIO is a logic output
column2: not a power source. Driving a 1 A motor from a 20 mA pin would do nothing or destroy the pin. The pin instead controls the gate (MOSFET) or base (BJT)

column1: What is a low-side switch?;The transistor sits between the load and ground. The load's top is tied to V+
column2: and the transistor connects or disconnects the load's bottom to ground.

column1: Why is a low-side switch easy to drive?;The transistor's source/emitter is at ground — the same reference as the MCU — so a 3.3 V GPIO directly provides the needed VGS (or base drive). No level shifting
What is the catch with a low-side switch?;When off
column2: the load's bottom floats but its top is still at V+ — the load is not fully de-energised
column3: just lacking a return path. A short from the load to ground would energise it without the MCU's consent.

column1: What is a high-side switch?;The transistor sits between V+ and the load (whose bottom is grounded). When on the load sees full supply
column2: and when off the load is at 0 V — genuinely disconnected from power.

column1: Why is a high-side switch wanted?;The load is truly off (zero volts across it)
column2: which is safer and mandatory in many systems — automotive especially
column3: where a low-side fault to chassis ground could turn something on unexpectedly.

column1: Why is a high-side NMOS switch hard to drive?;Its source is at the load (near V+ when conducting)
column2: so the gate must be driven above V+ (e.g. a 12 V rail needs ~22 V at the gate). A 3.3 V GPIO cannot do that — you need a gate driver with a charge pump (or a level shifter)
column3: or a P-channel device (driven below V+
column4: easier but worse on-resistance and cost).

column1: How do you use a BJT as a switch rather than an amplifier?;Drive it into saturation: supply enough base current that VCE(sat) collapses to a few tenths of a volt
column2: so it behaves like a closed switch. Enough means more than IC/β — you deliberately overdrive with a forced β of 10-20.

column1: Why must an inductive load have a flyback diode?;Switching off an inductive load (motor
column3: relay) makes the inductor try to keep current flowing
column4: generating a large reverse spike (v = L·di/dt) that destroys the transistor. A flyback diode across the load gives that current a safe path. Forgetting it is a common

column1: Low-side vs high-side: load state when off and drive difficulty.;Low-side: load floats with its top still at V+
column3: no extra circuitry. High-side: load truly at 0 V (safer)
column4: but the gate must exceed V+ so it needs a charge pump / gate driver / PMOS.

column2: what usually decides low-side vs high-side?;The electrical job is identical
column3: so the safety requirement chooses. Low-side is the cheap default
column4: but if rules require the load be provably de-energised when off (so a chassis short cannot run it)
column5: you need high-side and its harder gate drive.
